function.name: execute_code
function.arguments:
{"code":"
from hermes_tools import terminal
import hashlib
import json
import time

# Seed the MCP memory database with Project Avatar knowledge
# Organized by tags: project:avatar, project:avatar:category

db_path = "/Users/muadhsambul/.hermes/memories.db"

memories = [
    # === OVERVIEW ===
    {
        "content": "PROJECT AVATAR: LLM-driven autonomous drone controlled by natural language via MCP protocol. Location: ~/downloads/project-avatar. 43 Python source files, 35 test files (568 tests), 42 research docs. Phase 0.5 COMPLETE. Stage 0 (hardware sourcing) IN PROGRESS.",
        "tags": "project:avatar,overview",
        "memory_type": "project_overview"
    },
    # === ARCHITECTURE ===
    {
        "content": "Architecture 2.0: User -> Claude Code (Kimi K2.5 via Fireworks AI, 200 tok/s) -> Drone MCP Server (17+ tools, agent-agnostic) -> MAVSDK-Python -> PX4 Autopilot. Claude Code IS the agent, no separate Kimi client. SITL: udp://:14540. Hardware: serial:///dev/tty.usbmodemXXX:921600",
        "tags": "project:avatar,architecture",
        "memory_type": "architecture"
    },
    {
        "content": "4-layer safety architecture: Layer 1 PX4 Hard Reflexes (<100ms) -> Layer 2 Guardian Process (~10ms, 20Hz monitoring) -> Layer 3 LLM Reactions (1-3s) -> Layer 4 RC Override (human). Heartbeat MUST run on RPi not Mac. COM_OBL_RC_ACT=3 (RTL on offboard loss). COM_OF_LOSS_T=0.5s timeout.",
        "tags": "project:avatar,safety,architecture",
        "memory_type": "safety"
    },
    {
        "content": "MCP Server tools: Flight (arm_and_takeoff, goto_gps, fly_body_offset, set_velocity, hold, land, rtl, abort_mission) + Telemetry (get_telemetry, get_status, get_battery_status, get_health_status) + Vision (detect_objects, capture_frame, capture_and_detect) + Cinematic (execute_cinematic_shot, list_cinematic_templates, preview_cinematic_shot, track_target). 15 cinematic shot templates with PID control and latency compensation.",
        "tags": "project:avatar,architecture,mcp-tools",
        "memory_type": "tools"
    },
    # === KEY DECISIONS ===
    {
        "content": "Key decisions (see research/DECISIONS.md DEC-001 to DEC-020): MAVSDK-Python over ROS2 (2.8ms vs 7.1ms latency). Kimi K2.5 cloud LLM over local Llama 3 (200 tok/s vs 25-40). Agent-agnostic MCP server (not locked to any single agent). Phase 0.5 full SITL pre-validation before hardware. Hybrid vision: YOLO local 10FPS + Kimi cloud frames every 3-5s. Progressive confirmation: pre-flight + pre-arm + mid-flight exceptions.",
        "tags": "project:avatar,decisions",
        "memory_type": "decisions"
    },
    # === HARDWARE ===
    {
        "content": "Hardware plan: Mark4 7-inch frame + 2807 1300KV motors + 6S LiPo + Pixhawk 6C Mini flight controller + Raspberry Pi 4 (4GB) companion computer + Pi Camera 3 Wide. Budget $500-930 CAD depending on new vs used. External 5V/5A BEC needed (integrated is marginal). Shielded USB3 cables to avoid 2.4GHz EMI on WiFi.",
        "tags": "project:avatar,hardware",
        "memory_type": "hardware"
    },
    {
        "content": "Canadian drone law: Project Avatar ~900g AUW, exceeds 250g threshold. Must register ($5 at tc.canada.ca), get Basic Operations certificate (free online exam, 35 questions, 65% pass). Basic ops: VLOS, >30m from bystanders, uncontrolled airspace, max 122m altitude. Cannot fly within 5.6km of airports.",
        "tags": "project:avatar,legal,hardware",
        "memory_type": "legal"
    },
    # === ROADMAP ===
    {
        "content": "Roadmap: Phase 0.5 COMPLETE (all 47 tasks done, simulation validated). Stage 0 IN PROGRESS (hardware sourcing via Facebook Marketplace). Stage 1 Apr-May (Control Spine: hardware assembly, manual flight, MAVLink bridge, offboard heartbeat, Kimi integration). Stage 2 Jun-Jul (Vision: Pi Camera, YOLOv8+ByteTrack, State String). Stage 3 Aug-Sep (Depth: RealSense D435i, distance tools, bench only).",
        "tags": "project:avatar,roadmap",
        "memory_type": "roadmap"
    },
    # === CRITICAL OPEN WORK ===
    {
        "content": "CRITICAL OPEN WORK: 1) MCP compliance fixes (docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-12-mcp-compliance-fixes.md) - zero tool annotations, no output schemas, missing error codes, FlightTools not singleton, capture_frame uses base64 not ImageContent. 2) Demo video NOT recorded (script at avatar/scripts/demo_script.md). 3) 1 test failure: test_backward_compat test_wait_for_health_timeout. 4) Gap analyses exist for flight_tools, safety_arch, state_machine, mcp_standards.",
        "tags": "project:avatar,todo,open-work",
        "memory_type": "open_work"
    },
    # === KEY FILES ===
    {
        "content": "Key file locations: MCP server: avatar/mcp_server/server.py. Flight tools: avatar/mcp_server/tools/flight_tools.py. Cinematic: avatar/mcp_server/tools/cinematic_shots.py. Guardian: avatar/mav/guardian.py + guardian_async.py. Connection: avatar/mav/connection_manager.py. State machine: avatar/mav/state_machine.py. Research docs: research/ (42 .md files). Decisions log: research/DECISIONS.md. Master briefing: research/MASTER_BRIEFING.md (7000 lines, single source of truth).",
        "tags": "project:avatar,files",
        "memory_type": "reference"
    },
    # === PERFORMANCE ===
    {
        "content": "Performance targets and actuals: Kimi K2.5 inference ~1.2s (target <2s). MCP tool latency ~50ms (target <100ms). Vision 10 FPS mock (target 10-15 FPS). End-to-end command <2s. Heartbeat 20Hz on RPi. YOLO local inference via CPU on M3 (not MPS).",
        "tags": "project:avatar,performance",
        "memory_type": "performance"
    },
    # === HARD LIMITS ===
    {
        "content": "Hard safety limits (immutable, enforced by Guardian and PX4): Max altitude 120m AGL. Max distance 500m from home. Battery RTL at 25%. Battery land at 15%. Max speed 10 m/s. Geofence buffer 10m. These CANNOT be overridden by LLM or user commands.",
        "tags": "project:avatar,safety,limits",
        "memory_type": "safety_limits"
    },
    # === TESTING ===
    {
        "content": "Test suite: 568 tests collected. Unit tests (core, mav, mcp_server) ~363 pass excluding e2e/sitl/kimi. E2E tests require SITL running. Property-based tests via Hypothesis (coordinate transforms, safety validation). Coverage target 90%+ overall, 95%+ for safety-critical. Test markers: slow, integration, unit, e2e, property, safety_critical, hardware_in_loop.",
        "tags": "project:avatar,testing",
        "memory_type": "testing"
    },
    # === TECH STACK ===
    {
        "content": "Tech stack: Python 3.11+, asyncio-first. PX4 Autopilot v1.15.0. MAVSDK-Python (async API). MCP SDK (server + client). Kimi K2.5 via Fireworks AI (OpenAI-compatible API). YOLOv8-nano (ultralytics) for person detection. ByteTrack for ID persistence. Gazebo for SITL simulation. QGroundControl for PX4 config. Pi Camera Module 3 Wide for real vision. pyproject.toml configures mypy strict, pylint, bandit, ruff, pytest with hypothesis.",
        "tags": "project:avatar,tech-stack",
        "memory_type": "tech_stack"
    },
    # === CINEMATIC SYSTEM ===
    {
        "content": "Cinematic shot system: 15 pre-programmed templates for action sports (snowboarding, skateboarding, motocross, trail running). 6 motion curve types (LINEAR, EASE_IN_OUT, EASE_IN, EASE_OUT, EXPONENTIAL, BEZIER). LookaheadPredictor compensates for 150-250ms Pi 4 + YOLO vision latency. PID control for smooth distance maintenance. Hardware-aware: respects 15 m/s max speed, 5 m/s comfortable speed. Sport-specific profiles with height and speed presets.",
        "tags": "project:avatar,cinematic,features",
        "memory_type": "features"
    },
]

# Build SQL and insert
now = time.time()
from datetime import datetime
now_iso = datetime.utcnow().isoformat()

sql_commands = []
for mem in memories:
    content = mem["content"]
    content_hash = hashlib.sha256(content.encode()).hexdigest()
    tags_json = json.dumps(mem["tags"].split(","))
    metadata_json = json.dumps({"tags": mem["tags"], "type": mem["memory_type"]})
    memory_type = mem["memory_type"]
    
    sql_commands.append(
        f"INSERT OR IGNORE INTO memories (content_hash, content, tags, memory_type, metadata, created_at, updated_at, created_at_iso, updated_at_iso) VALUES ('{content_hash}', '{content.replace(chr(39), chr(39)+chr(39))}', '{tags_json}', '{memory_type}', '{metadata_json}', {now}, {now}, '{now_iso}', '{now_iso}');"
    )

# Write all SQL to a file and execute
sql_path = "/tmp/seed_avatar_memory.sql"
with open(sql_path, "w") as f:
    f.write("\n".join(sql_commands))

print(f"Generated {len(sql_commands)} memory insert statements")
print(f"SQL file: {sql_path}")
"}

name: native-mcp
description: Built-in MCP (Model Context Protocol) client that connects to external MCP servers, discovers their tools, and registers them as native Hermes Agent tools. Supports stdio and HTTP transports with automatic reconnection, security filtering, and zero-config tool injection.
version: 1.0.0
author: Hermes Agent
license: MIT
metadata:
  hermes:
    tags: [MCP, Tools, Integrations]
    related_skills: [mcporter]
---

# Native MCP Client

Hermes Agent has a built-in MCP client that connects to MCP servers at startup, discovers their tools, and makes them available as first-class tools the agent can call directly. No bridge CLI needed -- tools from MCP servers appear alongside built-in tools like `terminal`, `read_file`, etc.

## When to Use

Use this whenever you want to:
- Connect to MCP servers and use their tools from within Hermes Agent
- Add external capabilities (filesystem access, GitHub, databases, APIs) via MCP
- Run local stdio-based MCP servers (npx, uvx, or any command)
- Connect to remote HTTP/StreamableHTTP MCP servers
- Have MCP tools auto-discovered and available in every conversation

For ad-hoc, one-off MCP tool calls from the terminal without configuring anything, see the `mcporter` skill instead.

## Prerequisites

- **mcp Python package** -- optional dependency; install with `pip install mcp`. If not installed, MCP support is silently disabled.
- **Node.js** -- required for `npx`-based MCP servers (most community servers)
- **uv** -- required for `uvx`-based MCP servers (Python-based servers)

Install the MCP SDK:

```bash
pip install mcp
# or, if using uv:
uv pip install mcp
```

## Quick Start

Add MCP servers to `~/.hermes/config.yaml` under the `mcp_servers` key:

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  time:
    command: "uvx"
    args: ["mcp-server-time"]
```

Restart Hermes Agent. On startup it will:
1. Connect to the server
2. Discover available tools
3. Register them with the prefix `mcp_time_*`
4. Inject them into all platform toolsets

You can then use the tools naturally -- just ask the agent to get the current time.

## Configuration Reference

Each entry under `mcp_servers` is a server name mapped to its config. There are two transport types: **stdio** (command-based) and **HTTP** (url-based).

### Stdio Transport (command + args)

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  server_name:
    command: "npx"             # (required) executable to run
    args: ["-y", "pkg-name"]   # (optional) command arguments, default: []
    env:                       # (optional) environment variables for the subprocess
      SOME_API_KEY: "value"
    timeout: 120               # (optional) per-tool-call timeout in seconds, default: 120
    connect_timeout: 60        # (optional) initial connection timeout in seconds, default: 60
```

### HTTP Transport (url)

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  server_name:
    url: "https://my-server.example.com/mcp"   # (required) server URL
    headers:                                     # (optional) HTTP headers
      Authorization: "Bearer sk-..."
    timeout: 180               # (optional) per-tool-call timeout in seconds, default: 120
    connect_timeout: 60        # (optional) initial connection timeout in seconds, default: 60
```

### All Config Options

| Option            | Type   | Default | Description                                       |
|-------------------|--------|---------|---------------------------------------------------|
| `command`         | string | --      | Executable to run (stdio transport, required)     |
| `args`            | list   | `[]`    | Arguments passed to the command                   |
| `env`             | dict   | `{}`    | Extra environment variables for the subprocess    |
| `url`             | string | --      | Server URL (HTTP transport, required)             |
| `headers`         | dict   | `{}`    | HTTP headers sent with every request              |
| `timeout`         | int    | `120`   | Per-tool-call timeout in seconds                  |
| `connect_timeout` | int    | `60`    | Timeout for initial connection and discovery      |

Note: A server config must have either `command` (stdio) or `url` (HTTP), not both.

## How It Works

### Startup Discovery

When Hermes Agent starts, `discover_mcp_tools()` is called during tool initialization:

1. Reads `mcp_servers` from `~/.hermes/config.yaml`
2. For each server, spawns a connection in a dedicated background event loop
3. Initializes the MCP session and calls `list_tools()` to discover available tools
4. Registers each tool in the Hermes tool registry

### Tool Naming Convention

MCP tools are registered with the naming pattern:

```
mcp_{server_name}_{tool_name}
```

Hyphens and dots in names are replaced with underscores for LLM API compatibility.

Examples:
- Server `filesystem`, tool `read_file` → `mcp_filesystem_read_file`
- Server `github`, tool `list-issues` → `mcp_github_list_issues`
- Server `my-api`, tool `fetch.data` → `mcp_my_api_fetch_data`

### Auto-Injection

After discovery, MCP tools are automatically injected into all `hermes-*` platform toolsets (CLI, Discord, Telegram, etc.). This means MCP tools are available in every conversation without any additional configuration.

### Connection Lifecycle

- Each server runs as a long-lived asyncio Task in a background daemon thread
- Connections persist for the lifetime of the agent process
- If a connection drops, automatic reconnection with exponential backoff kicks in (up to 5 retries, max 60s backoff)
- On agent shutdown, all connections are gracefully closed

### Idempotency

`discover_mcp_tools()` is idempotent -- calling it multiple times only connects to servers that aren't already connected. Failed servers are retried on subsequent calls.

## Transport Types

### Stdio Transport

The most common transport. Hermes launches the MCP server as a subprocess and communicates over stdin/stdout.

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  filesystem:
    command: "npx"
    args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/home/user/projects"]
```

The subprocess inherits a **filtered** environment (see Security section below) plus any variables you specify in `env`.

### HTTP / StreamableHTTP Transport

For remote or shared MCP servers. Requires the `mcp` package to include HTTP client support (`mcp.client.streamable_http`).

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  remote_api:
    url: "https://mcp.example.com/mcp"
    headers:
      Authorization: "Bearer sk-..."
```

If HTTP support is not available in your installed `mcp` version, the server will fail with an ImportError and other servers will continue normally.

## Security

### Environment Variable Filtering

For stdio servers, Hermes does NOT pass your full shell environment to MCP subprocesses. Only safe baseline variables are inherited:

- `PATH`, `HOME`, `USER`, `LANG`, `LC_ALL`, `TERM`, `SHELL`, `TMPDIR`
- Any `XDG_*` variables

All other environment variables (API keys, tokens, secrets) are excluded unless you explicitly add them via the `env` config key. This prevents accidental credential leakage to untrusted MCP servers.

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  github:
    command: "npx"
    args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"]
    env:
      # Only this token is passed to the subprocess
      GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN: "ghp_..."
```

### Credential Stripping in Error Messages

If an MCP tool call fails, any credential-like patterns in the error message are automatically redacted before being shown to the LLM. This covers:

- GitHub PATs (`ghp_...`)
- OpenAI-style keys (`sk-...`)
- Bearer tokens
- Generic `token=`, `key=`, `API_KEY=`, `password=`, `secret=` patterns

## Troubleshooting

### "MCP SDK not available -- skipping MCP tool discovery"

The `mcp` Python package is not installed. Install it:

```bash
pip install mcp
```

### "No MCP servers configured"

No `mcp_servers` key in `~/.hermes/config.yaml`, or it's empty. Add at least one server.

### "Failed to connect to MCP server 'X'"

Common causes:
- **Command not found**: The `command` binary isn't on PATH. Ensure `npx`, `uvx`, or the relevant command is installed.
- **Package not found**: For npx servers, the npm package may not exist or may need `-y` in args to auto-install.
- **Timeout**: The server took too long to start. Increase `connect_timeout`.
- **Port conflict**: For HTTP servers, the URL may be unreachable.

### "MCP server 'X' requires HTTP transport but mcp.client.streamable_http is not available"

Your `mcp` package version doesn't include HTTP client support. Upgrade:

```bash
pip install --upgrade mcp
```

### Tools not appearing

- Check that the server is listed under `mcp_servers` (not `mcp` or `servers`)
- Ensure the YAML indentation is correct
- Look at Hermes Agent startup logs for connection messages
- Tool names are prefixed with `mcp_{server}_{tool}` -- look for that pattern

### Connection keeps dropping

The client retries up to 5 times with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, capped at 60s). If the server is fundamentally unreachable, it gives up after 5 attempts. Check the server process and network connectivity.

## Examples

### Time Server (uvx)

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  time:
    command: "uvx"
    args: ["mcp-server-time"]
```

Registers tools like `mcp_time_get_current_time`.

### Filesystem Server (npx)

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  filesystem:
    command: "npx"
    args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/home/user/documents"]
    timeout: 30
```

Registers tools like `mcp_filesystem_read_file`, `mcp_filesystem_write_file`, `mcp_filesystem_list_directory`.

### GitHub Server with Authentication

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  github:
    command: "npx"
    args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"]
    env:
      GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN: "ghp_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx"
    timeout: 60
```

Registers tools like `mcp_github_list_issues`, `mcp_github_create_pull_request`, etc.

### Remote HTTP Server

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  company_api:
    url: "https://mcp.mycompany.com/v1/mcp"
    headers:
      Authorization: "Bearer sk-xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx"
      X-Team-Id: "engineering"
    timeout: 180
    connect_timeout: 30
```

### Multiple Servers

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  time:
    command: "uvx"
    args: ["mcp-server-time"]

  filesystem:
    command: "npx"
    args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/tmp"]

  github:
    command: "npx"
    args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"]
    env:
      GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN: "ghp_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx"

  company_api:
    url: "https://mcp.internal.company.com/mcp"
    headers:
      Authorization: "Bearer sk-xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx"
    timeout: 300
```

All tools from all servers are registered and available simultaneously. Each server's tools are prefixed with its name to avoid collisions.

## Sampling (Server-Initiated LLM Requests)

Hermes supports MCP's `sampling/createMessage` capability — MCP servers can request LLM completions through the agent during tool execution. This enables agent-in-the-loop workflows (data analysis, content generation, decision-making).

Sampling is **enabled by default**. Configure per server:

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  my_server:
    command: "npx"
    args: ["-y", "my-mcp-server"]
    sampling:
      enabled: true           # default: true
      model: "gemini-3-flash" # model override (optional)
      max_tokens_cap: 4096    # max tokens per request
      timeout: 30             # LLM call timeout (seconds)
      max_rpm: 10             # max requests per minute
      allowed_models: []      # model whitelist (empty = all)
      max_tool_rounds: 5      # tool loop limit (0 = disable)
      log_level: "info"       # audit verbosity
```

Servers can also include `tools` in sampling requests for multi-turn tool-augmented workflows. The `max_tool_rounds` config prevents infinite tool loops. Per-server audit metrics (requests, errors, tokens, tool use count) are tracked via `get_mcp_status()`.

Disable sampling for untrusted servers with `sampling: { enabled: false }`.

## Installation Pitfalls

### Python MCP servers (pip install)
- `pip` may not be on PATH. Always use the full python path from a venv:
  ```bash
  /path/to/venv/bin/python -m pip install <package>
  ```
- In config, use the **absolute venv python path** as `command`, NOT just `python`:
  ```yaml
  command: "/Users/user/project/.venv/bin/python"
  args: ["-m", "mcp_memory_service"]
  ```
- Reason: Hermes does NOT activate venvs. Bare `python` resolves to system python which won't have the package.

### Node.js MCP servers (npm install -g)
- `npm install -g` fails without sudo on macOS. Use `--prefix ~/.local` instead:
  ```bash
  npm install -g <package> --prefix ~/.local
  ```
- Binary lands at `~/.local/bin/<package-name>`
- Ensure `~/.local/bin` is on PATH, or use absolute path in config

### npx-based servers
- Most community servers use `npx -y <package>` which auto-downloads each time
- First call can be slow (downloading). Add `-y` to args to skip confirmation.

## Recommended Servers

### Memory Server (mcp-memory-service)
Best local memory server. SQLite-vec + sentence-transformers (all-MiniLM-L6-v2). Zero cloud dependency.

```bash
/path/to/venv/bin/python -m pip install mcp-memory-service
```

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  memory:
    command: "/path/to/venv/bin/python"
    args: ["-m", "mcp_memory_service"]
    env:
      MCP_MEMORY_SQLITE_PATH: "/Users/user/.hermes/memories.db"
    timeout: 30
```

Tools: store_memory, search_by_tag, retrieve_memory, delete_memory, check_database_health

### Comet MCP (Perplexity via browser)
Connects to Perplexity's Comet browser via CDP. Uses existing Perplexity subscription -- no API key needed.

```bash
npm install -g comet-mcp --prefix ~/.local
```

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  comet:
    command: "/Users/user/.local/bin/comet-mcp"
    timeout: 120
```

Prerequisite: Comet browser must be installed (from perplexity.ai/comet). Server auto-launches it with remote debugging.

Tools: comet_connect, comet_ask, comet_poll, comet_stop, comet_screenshot, comet_mode

Use cases: Deep research via Perplexity subscription, web search with AI synthesis, screenshots of web pages.

### Official Memory Server (TypeScript, simplest)
Knowledge graph model (entities + relations + observations). Zero config.

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  memory:
    command: "npx"
    args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-memory"]
```

Tools: create_entities, create_relations, add_observations, search_nodes, open_nodes, read_graph, delete_entities/observations/relations

## Notes

- MCP tools are called synchronously from the agent's perspective but run asynchronously on a dedicated background event loop
- Tool results are returned as JSON with either `{"result": "..."}` or `{"error": "..."}`
- The native MCP client is independent of `mcporter` -- you can use both simultaneously
- Server connections are persistent and shared across all conversations in the same agent process
- Adding or removing servers requires restarting the agent (no hot-reload currently)

# mcporter

Use `mcporter` to discover, call, and manage [MCP (Model Context Protocol)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/) servers and tools directly from the terminal.

## Prerequisites

Requires Node.js:
```bash
# No install needed (runs via npx)
npx mcporter list

# Or install globally
npm install -g mcporter
```

## Quick Start

```bash
# List MCP servers already configured on this machine
mcporter list

# List tools for a specific server with schema details
mcporter list <server> --schema

# Call a tool
mcporter call <server.tool> key=value
```

## Discovering MCP Servers

mcporter auto-discovers servers configured by other MCP clients (Claude Desktop, Cursor, etc.) on the machine. To find new servers to use, browse registries like [mcpfinder.dev](https://mcpfinder.dev) or [mcp.so](https://mcp.so), then connect ad-hoc:

```bash
# Connect to any MCP server by URL (no config needed)
mcporter list --http-url https://some-mcp-server.com --name my_server

# Or run a stdio server on the fly
mcporter list --stdio "npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem" --name fs
```

## Calling Tools

```bash
# Key=value syntax
mcporter call linear.list_issues team=ENG limit:5

# Function syntax
mcporter call "linear.create_issue(title: \"Bug fix needed\")"

# Ad-hoc HTTP server (no config needed)
mcporter call https://api.example.com/mcp.fetch url=https://example.com

# Ad-hoc stdio server
mcporter call --stdio "bun run ./server.ts" scrape url=https://example.com

# JSON payload
mcporter call <server.tool> --args '{"limit": 5}'

# Machine-readable output (recommended for Hermes)
mcporter call <server.tool> key=value --output json
```

## Auth and Config

```bash
# OAuth login for a server
mcporter auth <server | url> [--reset]

# Manage config
mcporter config list
mcporter config get <key>
mcporter config add <server>
mcporter config remove <server>
mcporter config import <path>
```

Config file location: `./config/mcporter.json` (override with `--config`).

## Daemon

For persistent server connections:
```bash
mcporter daemon start
mcporter daemon status
mcporter daemon stop
mcporter daemon restart
```

## Code Generation

```bash
# Generate a CLI wrapper for an MCP server
mcporter generate-cli --server <name>
mcporter generate-cli --command <url>

# Inspect a generated CLI
mcporter inspect-cli <path> [--json]

# Generate TypeScript types/client
mcporter emit-ts <server> --mode client
mcporter emit-ts <server> --mode types
```

## Notes

- Use `--output json` for structured output that's easier to parse
- Ad-hoc servers (HTTP URL or `--stdio` command) work without any config — useful for one-off calls
- OAuth auth may require interactive browser flow — use `terminal(command="mcporter auth <server>", pty=true)` if needed

                created_at=now,

{"success": true, "name": "project-documentation-ingestion", "description": "Bulk-read all project documentation files in parallel and consolidate key findings into memory. Use for project onboarding, deep analysis, or knowledge capture.", "tags": [], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: project-documentation-ingestion
description: Bulk-read all project documentation files in parallel and consolidate key findings into memory. Use for project onboarding, deep analysis, or knowledge capture.
---

# Project Documentation Ingestion

## When to Use

- User asks to "read all docs", "analyze all files", "deep dive into the project"
- Onboarding to a new project you haven't worked with before
- Capturing project state before a major change
- User says something like "read every document and summarize"

## Steps

### 1. Inventory All Project Docs

```bash
find . -name "*.md" -not -path "./.git/*" -not -path "./.venv/*" -not -path "./venv/*" -not -path "./node_modules/*" -not -path "./.pytest_cache/*" | sort
```

Exclude noise directories (vendor, venv, .git, node_modules, build artifacts). Count the files to determine batch sizing.

### 2. Split Into Batches for Parallel Subagents

Split into 3 batches of roughly equal size (~20-25 files each). Use `delegate_task` with `tasks` array (batch mode) for parallel execution.

Each subagent task should specify:
- **goal**: Read and summarize ALL listed .md files. For each file provide: filename, 1-2 sentence summary, key facts/specs/decisions, actionable items.
- **context**: Brief project description + working directory path. State that files should be read with `read_file` tool.
- **toolsets**: `["file"]` only -- subagents just need to read files.

### 3. Prompt Template Per Subagent

```
Read and summarize ALL of the following .md files for [PROJECT NAME]. For each file, provide: filename, 1-2 sentence summary of what it contains, key facts/specs/decisions found, and any actionable items or open tasks mentioned. Be thorough - extract every important detail. Files to read:

1. ./path/to/file1.md
2. ./path/to/file2.md
...

Return a structured summary for each file.
```

### 4. Consolidate Into Memory

After subagents return, extract the most durable facts and save to memory:
- Architecture and tech stack (concise)
- Current phase/status and roadmap
- Critical open work and blockers
- Key file references for future lookups

**Memory management**: Memory has a character limit (~2200 chars). If you hit it:
1. Remove the older/less critical entry
2. Merge entries into a single denser entry
3. Prioritize: architecture + current status + open work over detailed specs

### 5. Pitfalls

- **File count**: Projects can have 50-100+ .md files. Always count first and split appropriately.
- **Large files**: Some docs are 1000+ lines. Subagents handle this fine but it takes time.
- **Memory limits**: Don't try to store everything. Summarize aggressively. Key refs (file paths) matter more than full content.
- **Exclude vendor docs**: .venv/, node_modules/, build/ directories contain license files and third-party READMEs -- skip them.
- **Subagent toolsets**: Only give `["file"]` -- they don't need browser, terminal, or search.

## Time Budget

- Inventory: ~30 seconds
- Subagent dispatch: ~10 seconds
- Parallel reading (3 batches of ~25 files): ~3-4 minutes
- Memory consolidation: ~1-2 minutes
- **Total: ~5-7 minutes** for a 60-80 file project

# Subagent-Driven Development

## Overview

Execute implementation plans by dispatching fresh subagents per task with systematic two-stage review.

**Core principle:** Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review (spec then quality) = high quality, fast iteration.

## When to Use

Use this skill when:
- You have an implementation plan (from writing-plans skill or user requirements)
- Tasks are mostly independent
- Quality and spec compliance are important
- You want automated review between tasks

**vs. manual execution:**
- Fresh context per task (no confusion from accumulated state)
- Automated review process catches issues early
- Consistent quality checks across all tasks
- Subagents can ask questions before starting work

## The Process

### 1. Read and Parse Plan

Read the plan file. Extract ALL tasks with their full text and context upfront. Create a todo list:

```python
# Read the plan
read_file("docs/plans/feature-plan.md")

# Create todo list with all tasks
todo([
    {"id": "task-1", "content": "Create User model with email field", "status": "pending"},
    {"id": "task-2", "content": "Add password hashing utility", "status": "pending"},
    {"id": "task-3", "content": "Create login endpoint", "status": "pending"},
])
```

**Key:** Read the plan ONCE. Extract everything. Don't make subagents read the plan file — provide the full task text directly in context.

### 2. Per-Task Workflow

For EACH task in the plan:

#### Step 1: Dispatch Implementer Subagent

Use `delegate_task` with complete context:

```python
delegate_task(
    goal="Implement Task 1: Create User model with email and password_hash fields",
    context="""
    TASK FROM PLAN:
    - Create: src/models/user.py
    - Add User class with email (str) and password_hash (str) fields
    - Use bcrypt for password hashing
    - Include __repr__ for debugging

    FOLLOW TDD:
    1. Write failing test in tests/models/test_user.py
    2. Run: pytest tests/models/test_user.py -v (verify FAIL)
    3. Write minimal implementation
    4. Run: pytest tests/models/test_user.py -v (verify PASS)
    5. Run: pytest tests/ -q (verify no regressions)
    6. Commit: git add -A && git commit -m "feat: add User model with password hashing"

    PROJECT CONTEXT:
    - Python 3.11, Flask app in src/app.py
    - Existing models in src/models/
    - Tests use pytest, run from project root
    - bcrypt already in requirements.txt
    """,
    toolsets=['terminal', 'file']
)
```

#### Step 2: Dispatch Spec Compliance Reviewer

After the implementer completes, verify against the original spec:

```python
delegate_task(
    goal="Review if implementation matches the spec from the plan",
    context="""
    ORIGINAL TASK SPEC:
    - Create src/models/user.py with User class
    - Fields: email (str), password_hash (str)
    - Use bcrypt for password hashing
    - Include __repr__

    CHECK:
    - [ ] All requirements from spec implemented?
    - [ ] File paths match spec?
    - [ ] Function signatures match spec?
    - [ ] Behavior matches expected?
    - [ ] Nothing extra added (no scope creep)?

    OUTPUT: PASS or list of specific spec gaps to fix.
    """,
    toolsets=['file']
)
```

**If spec issues found:** Fix gaps, then re-run spec review. Continue only when spec-compliant.

#### Step 3: Dispatch Code Quality Reviewer

After spec compliance passes:

```python
delegate_task(
    goal="Review code quality for Task 1 implementation",
    context="""
    FILES TO REVIEW:
    - src/models/user.py
    - tests/models/test_user.py

    CHECK:
    - [ ] Follows project conventions and style?
    - [ ] Proper error handling?
    - [ ] Clear variable/function names?
    - [ ] Adequate test coverage?
    - [ ] No obvious bugs or missed edge cases?
    - [ ] No security issues?

    OUTPUT FORMAT:
    - Critical Issues: [must fix before proceeding]
    - Important Issues: [should fix]
    - Minor Issues: [optional]
    - Verdict: APPROVED or REQUEST_CHANGES
    """,
    toolsets=['file']
)
```

**If quality issues found:** Fix issues, re-review. Continue only when approved.

#### Step 4: Mark Complete

```python
todo([{"id": "task-1", "content": "Create User model with email field", "status": "completed"}], merge=True)
```

### 3. Final Review

After ALL tasks are complete, dispatch a final integration reviewer:

```python
delegate_task(
    goal="Review the entire implementation for consistency and integration issues",
    context="""
    All tasks from the plan are complete. Review the full implementation:
    - Do all components work together?
    - Any inconsistencies between tasks?
    - All tests passing?
    - Ready for merge?
    """,
    toolsets=['terminal', 'file']
)
```

### 4. Verify and Commit

```bash
# Run full test suite
pytest tests/ -q

# Review all changes
git diff --stat

# Final commit if needed
git add -A && git commit -m "feat: complete [feature name] implementation"
```

## Task Granularity

**Each task = 2-5 minutes of focused work.**

**Too big:**
- "Implement user authentication system"

**Right size:**
- "Create User model with email and password fields"
- "Add password hashing function"
- "Create login endpoint"
- "Add JWT token generation"
- "Create registration endpoint"

## Red Flags — Never Do These

- Start implementation without a plan
- Skip reviews (spec compliance OR code quality)
- Proceed with unfixed critical/important issues
- Dispatch multiple implementation subagents for tasks that touch the same files
- Make subagent read the plan file (provide full text in context instead)
- Skip scene-setting context (subagent needs to understand where the task fits)
- Ignore subagent questions (answer before letting them proceed)
- Accept "close enough" on spec compliance
- Skip review loops (reviewer found issues → implementer fixes → review again)
- Let implementer self-review replace actual review (both are needed)
- **Start code quality review before spec compliance is PASS** (wrong order)
- Move to next task while either review has open issues

## Handling Issues

### If Subagent Asks Questions

- Answer clearly and completely
- Provide additional context if needed
- Don't rush them into implementation

### If Reviewer Finds Issues

- Implementer subagent (or a new one) fixes them
- Reviewer reviews again
- Repeat until approved
- Don't skip the re-review

### If Subagent Fails a Task

- Dispatch a new fix subagent with specific instructions about what went wrong
- Don't try to fix manually in the controller session (context pollution)

## Efficiency Notes

**Why fresh subagent per task:**
- Prevents context pollution from accumulated state
- Each subagent gets clean, focused context
- No confusion from prior tasks' code or reasoning

**Why two-stage review:**
- Spec review catches under/over-building early
- Quality review ensures the implementation is well-built
- Catches issues before they compound across tasks

**Cost trade-off:**
- More subagent invocations (implementer + 2 reviewers per task)
- But catches issues early (cheaper than debugging compounded problems later)

## Integration with Other Skills

### With writing-plans

This skill EXECUTES plans created by the writing-plans skill:
1. User requirements → writing-plans → implementation plan
2. Implementation plan → subagent-driven-development → working code

### With test-driven-development

Implementer subagents should follow TDD:
1. Write failing test first
2. Implement minimal code
3. Verify test passes
4. Commit

Include TDD instructions in every implementer context.

### With requesting-code-review

The two-stage review process IS the code review. For final integration review, use the requesting-code-review skill's review dimensions.

### With systematic-debugging

If a subagent encounters bugs during implementation:
1. Follow systematic-debugging process
2. Find root cause before fixing
3. Write regression test
4. Resume implementation

## Example Workflow

```
[Read plan: docs/plans/auth-feature.md]
[Create todo list with 5 tasks]

--- Task 1: Create User model ---
[Dispatch implementer subagent]
  Implementer: "Should email be unique?"
  You: "Yes, email must be unique"
  Implementer: Implemented, 3/3 tests passing, committed.

[Dispatch spec reviewer]
  Spec reviewer: ✅ PASS — all requirements met

[Dispatch quality reviewer]
  Quality reviewer: ✅ APPROVED — clean code, good tests

[Mark Task 1 complete]

--- Task 2: Password hashing ---
[Dispatch implementer subagent]
  Implementer: No questions, implemented, 5/5 tests passing.

[Dispatch spec reviewer]
  Spec reviewer: ❌ Missing: password strength validation (spec says "min 8 chars")

[Implementer fixes]
  Implementer: Added validation, 7/7 tests passing.

[Dispatch spec reviewer again]
  Spec reviewer: ✅ PASS

[Dispatch quality reviewer]
  Quality reviewer: Important: Magic number 8, extract to constant
  Implementer: Extracted MIN_PASSWORD_LENGTH constant
  Quality reviewer: ✅ APPROVED

[Mark Task 2 complete]

... (continue for all tasks)

[After all tasks: dispatch final integration reviewer]
[Run full test suite: all passing]
[Done!]
```

## Remember

```
Fresh subagent per task
Two-stage review every time
Spec compliance FIRST
Code quality SECOND
Never skip reviews
Catch issues early
```

**Quality is not an accident. It's the result of systematic process.**

messages.content:
{"results": [{"task_index": 0, "status": "completed", "summary": "I now have comprehensive data from all 15 files. Here is the structured summary:

---

## COMPREHENSIVE SUMMARY: Project Avatar MCP Server

### OVERVIEW
Project Avatar is an LLM-driven autonomous drone control system that uses the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to expose drone control tools to AI agents (Claude Code, OpenCode, etc.). The MCP server communicates via JSON-RPC over stdio and controls a PX4-based drone via MAVSDK.

---

### FILE-BY-FILE ANALYSIS

#### 1. `__init__.py` (250 lines)
- **Role**: Package public API definition with re-exports
- **Key exports**: `AvatarMCPServer`, `AvatarMCPServerConfig`, 6 Protocol classes, 13 legacy compat symbols
- **Pattern**: Lazy loading with `try/except ImportError` for MCP module - sets `_SERVER_AVAILABLE` flag
- **Protocols exported**: `ConfirmationProviderProtocol`, `FlightStateMachineProtocol`, `GuardianProcessProtocol`, `TelemetryBroadcasterProtocol`, `ToolHandlerProtocol`, `ToolRegistryProtocol`

#### 2. `__main__.py` (174 lines)
- **Role**: Entry point for `python -m avatar.mcp_server`
- **Key function**: None in file itself -- calls `asyncio.run(main())` which delegates to `server.main()`
- **Pattern**: Calculates project root from `__file__` path, inserts into `sys.path`

#### 3. `compat.py` (890 lines)
- **Role**: Backward compatibility shim layer (deprecated, removal in v0.4.0)
- **Key classes**:
  - `DroneConnection` -- wraps `ConnectionManager` singleton
  - `DroneMCPServer` -- wraps `AvatarMCPServer` via `__getattr__` delegation
  - `DroneMCPServerConfig` -- dataclass converting to `FlightToolsConfig`
- **Legacy functions** (all with `drone_id` param, all delegate to new API):
  - `arm(drone_id="default") -> Dict[str, Any]`
  - `takeoff(altitude: float) -> Dict[str, Any]`
  - `arm_and_takeoff_legacy(altitude_m=10.0, drone_id="default") -> Dict[str, Any]`
  - `get_telemetry(drone_id="default") -> Dict[str, Any]`
  - `land_legacy(drone_id="default") -> Dict[str, Any]`
  - `rtl_legacy(drone_id="default") -> Dict[str, Any]`
  - `abort_mission_legacy(reason="", drone_id="default") -> Dict[str, Any]`
- **Utilities**: `check_api_compatibility() -> Dict`, `get_migration_guide() -> str`
- **Pattern**: Deprecation warning deduplication via `_warned_classes`/`_warned_functions` sets

#### 4. `confirmation.py` (1235 lines)
- **Role**: Human-in-the-loop confirmation for critical drone operations
- **Enums**:
  - `ConfirmationResponse(str, Enum)`: YES, NO, ABORT, TIMEOUT
  - `ExceptionType(str, Enum)`: PERSON_DETECTED, OBSTACLE_DETECTED, NO_FLY_ZONE, LOW_BATTERY, WEATHER_WARNING, GPS_DEGRADATION, CUSTOM
- **Dataclasses**:
  - `MissionPlan`: waypoints, estimated_duration_s, max_altitude_m, max_distance_m, description
  - `TelemetrySnapshot`: position, battery_percent, gps_fix, satellite_count, flight_mode, armed, in_air
  - `ConfirmationConfig`: timeout_s=10.0, show_telemetry_details=True, require_explicit_abort=False
- **Key class**: `ConfirmationManager`
  - `__init__(config: Optional[ConfirmationConfig] = None)`
  - `pre_flight_confirmation(plan: MissionPlan) -> bool` (Level 1)
  - `pre_arm_confirmation(telemetry: TelemetrySnapshot) -> bool` (Level 2)
  - `exception_confirmation(exception_type: ExceptionType, context: Optional[dict]) -> str` (Level 3)
  - `timeout_confirmation(default_action: str) -> str` (Level 4)
  - `submit_response(response: str) -> None` (programmatic response injection)
  - Internal: `_request_confirmation()`, `_get_user_input()`, `_format_pre_flight_message()`, `_format_pre_arm_message()`, `_format_exception_message()`
- **Pattern**: 4-level progressive confirmation; asyncio.Queue for thread-safe responses; fail-safe defaults (always "no" on timeout)

#### 5. `protocols.py` (757 lines)
- **Role**: Runtime-checkable Protocol classes for type safety
- **Protocols** (all `@runtime_checkable`):
  - `ToolHandlerProtocol`: `async __call__(**kwargs) -> dict`, `name -> str`, `description -> str`
  - `ToolRegistryProtocol`: `register(tool)`, `get(name) -> Optional`, `list_tools() -> list[str]`, `unregister(name) -> bool`
  - `ConfirmationProviderProtocol`: `request_confirmation(context) -> bool`, `is_confirmation_required(operation) -> bool`, `get_risk_level(operation) -> str`
  - `TelemetryBroadcasterProtocol`: `start_broadcast(interval_s)`, `stop_broadcast()`, `get_latest_telemetry() -> dict`, `is_broadcasting() -> bool`
  - `GuardianProcessProtocol`: `validate(operation, params) -> tuple[bool, str]`, `is_operation_allowed(state, operation) -> bool`
  - `FlightStateMachineProtocol`: `current_state -> str`, `transition_to(new_state, context) -> bool`, `is_transition_allowed(from, to) -> bool`, `get_allowed_operations(state) -> list[str]`

#### 6. `server.py` (2001 lines) -- THE CORE
- **Role**: Main MCP server class, tool registration, request routing
- **Config**: `AvatarMCPServerConfig` dataclass:
  - system_address="udp://:14540", connection_timeout_s=30.0, telemetry_refresh_ms=100, heartbeat_hz=20.0, enable_guardian=True, enable_auto_failsafe=True, max_retries=3, retry_delay_s=1.0
- **Key class**: `AvatarMCPServer`
  - `__init__(config)` -- creates Server("avatar-mcp"), ConnectionManager, TelemetryCache, HeartbeatService, FlightStateMachine, AsyncGuardian, FlightTools
  - `_setup_handlers()` -- registers `list_tools` and `call_tool` MCP handlers
  - `initialize() -> bool` -- 5-step startup: connect, telemetry cache, heartbeat, state machine, guardian
  - `run()` -- stdio MCP transport, blocks until shutdown
  - `shutdown()` -- reverse-order cleanup
  - `_route_tool(name, arguments) -> str` -- dispatch to tool functions
  - `get_status() -> Dict` -- comprehensive server status
  - Async context manager: `__aenter__`/`__aexit__`
- **27 MCP Tools Registered** with full JSON Schema input schemas:

| Tool Name | Required Params | Optional Params | Category |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|----------|
| `arm_and_takeoff` | - | altitude_m (1-120, default 10) | Flight |
| `get_telemetry` | - | - | Telemetry |
| `land` | - | - | Flight |
| `rtl` | - | - | Flight |
| `abort_mission` | - | reason (string) | Flight |
| `goto_gps` | lat, lon | alt_m, speed_ms (0.1-15) | Flight |
| `fly_body_offset` | - | forward_m, right_m, up_m, yaw_align, speed_m_s | Flight |
| `set_velocity` | - | north_m_s, east_m_s, down_m_s, yaw_deg, duration_s (0.1-10) | Flight |
| `hold` | - | duration_s (1-60), position_tolerance_m (0.1-10), auto_rtl_on_drift | Flight |
| `detect_objects` | - | confidence_threshold (0-1) | Vision |
| `get_detected_objects` | - | - | Vision |
| `get_status` | - | - | Status |
| `front_flip` | - | - | Acrobatics |
| `back_flip` | - | - | Acrobatics |
| `barrel_roll` | - | direction ("left"/"right") | Acrobatics |
| `yaw_spin` | - | direction ("cw"/"ccw"), rotations (0.5-5) | Acrobatics |
| `loop_maneuver` | - | - | Acrobatics |
| `corkscrew` | - | rotations (0.5-3) | Acrobatics |
| `set_gimbal` | - | pitch_deg (-90 to 30), yaw_deg (-180 to 180), roll_deg (-45 to 45) | Tracking |
| `point_camera_at` | lat, lon | alt_m | Tracking |
| `orbit_target` | target_lat, target_lon | target_alt_m, radius_m (5-100), speed_m_s (1-10), altitude_offset_m (10-50), clockwise, duration_s (5-300), keep_camera_locked | Tracking |
| `track_target` | target_lat, target_lon | target_velocity_north/east, follow_distance_m (5-30), altitude_m (15-50), speed_m_s (3-15), duration_s, predictive, tracking_mode ("follow"/"lead"/"side") | Tracking |
| `spiral_search` | center_lat, center_lon | start_altitude_m, max_radius_m, max_altitude_m, rotations, speed_m_s | Tracking |
| `execute_cinematic_shot` | template_name, target_lat, target_lon | target_alt_m, duration_s, custom_params | Cinematic |
| `list_cinematic_templates` | - | - | Cinematic |
| `preview_cinematic_shot` | template_name, target_lat, target_lon | - | Cinematic |

- **Error handling**: All exceptions in `handle_call_tool` are caught, logged, and returned as `{"success": false, "error": str(e)}` wrapped in `TextContent`
- **Return type**: All tools return JSON strings wrapped in `List[types.TextContent]`
- **MCP protocol**: Server name "avatar-mcp", version "0.2.0", stdio transport
- **Legacy alias**: `DroneMCPServer(AvatarMCPServer)` with deprecation warning

#### 7. `tools/__init__.py` (379 lines)
- **Role**: Re-exports from all tool submodules, organized into 6 categories
- **Re-exports**: FlightTools, FlightToolsConfig, 12 flight functions; VisionTools, VisionToolsConfig, capture_frame, get_detected_objects; TelemetryTools, TelemetryToolsConfig, 7 telemetry functions; 7 acrobatic functions; 5 tracking functions; 3 cinematic functions
- **`__all__`**: 39 symbols total

#### 8. `flight_tools.py` (2023 lines)
- **Config**: `FlightToolsConfig` dataclass (system_address, max_retries, retry_delay_s, health_timeout_s, default_takeoff_altitude_m=10.0, default_goto_speed_m_s=5.0, default_body_offset_speed_m_s=5.0)
- **Key class**: `FlightTools`

#### 14. `advanced_tracking.py` (1326 lines)
- **Role**: Kalman filter-based tracking with acceleration modeling
- **Key dataclass**: `TrackingState` (x, y, z, vx, vy, vz, ax, ay, az, timestamp, confidence) -- 9-dimensional state vector
- **Key class**: `KalmanTracker`
  - `__init__(dt=0.1)` -- 9-state Kalman filter: state=[x,y,z,vx,vy,vz,ax,ay,az]
  - State transition matrix F: constant acceleration model with `position += velocity*dt + 0.5*accel*dt^2`
  - Process noise Q: position=0.1, velocity=1.0, acceleration=5.0 (high for human agility)
  - Measurement noise R: 2.0m (standard GPS)
  - Measurement matrix H: 3x9 (only measures position)
  - `update(x, y, z, timestamp) -> TrackingState` -- full predict+correct cycle
  - `predict(horizon_s) -> (x, y, z)` -- full kinematic: `x + v*t + 0.5*a*t^2`
  - Variable dt support (adapts F matrix for jitter)
- **Gimbal coordination**: Rate limiting for smooth camera slewing
- **Advantage over simple predictor**: Models acceleration, correctly predicts jump arcs and braking

### KEY ARCHITECTURAL PATTERNS

1. **Singleton ConnectionManager**: All tools share one MAVSDK connection
2. **TelemetryCache**: 100ms refresh, non-blocking, stale data detection
3. **HeartbeatService**: 20Hz emission for PX4 offboard mode
4. **FlightStateMachine**: Validated state transitions (DISARMED -> ARMED -> TAKEOFF -> FLYING -> LANDING)
5. **AsyncGuardian**: Concurrent safety monitoring (geofence, altitude, speed, battery)
6. **Two-phase tool registration**: Schema declaration via `list_tools`, execution via `call_tool` -> `_route_tool`
7. **Module-level wrapper functions**: Each tool has both a class method (stateful) and module-level async function (stateless, returns JSON string for MCP)
8. **Error handling**: All errors caught and returned as `{"success": false, "error": "..."}` JSON; server never crashes from tool errors
9. **Confirmation flow**: 4-level progressive safety (pre-flight -> pre-arm -> exception -> timeout)
10. **Lookahead prediction**: Compensates for 200ms vision latency in tracking
11. **Kalman filter**: 9-state filter for acceleration-aware subject tracking (advanced_tracking.py)

**Summary**: Comprehensive Python code quality standards for safety-critical drone software, targeting SIL-2 equivalent compliance with DO-178C, ISO/IEC 61508, and ASTM F3269.

- Tool stack: black (88 char lines), isort, pylint (complexity limits: max 15 branches, 60 statements), bandit (security), pydocstyle, codespell
- Pre-commit hooks include custom safety checks: `no-assert-in-production`, `check-safety-comments`
- Asserts for internal invariants only; exceptions for all external/safety-critical validation
- Custom validation framework: Range, Pattern, validate() decorator with drone-specific validators
- SafetyTimeout decorator (signal-based + threading fallback), TimeoutContext manager
- Resource cleanup patterns (MotorResource with guaranteed disarm on exit)
- HardLimits dataclass (frozen=True) with max_altitude=120m, max_distance=500m, min_battery_rtl=25%
- Custom exception hierarchy: SafetyError, StateError, NavigationError, ValidationError
- Result types (Ok/Err) instead of exceptions for flight-critical returns
- NewTypes for domain primitives: DroneId, AltitudeMeters, VelocityMPS, TimestampNS

  - Stage 3: Depth & Payload ("Hands") - FR 3.1-3.5 (RealSense D435i, depth-augmented State String, distance-gated tools, ESP32 payload, bench-first policy)

- **Hard limits**: max altitude 120m AMSL, max distance 500m, min battery RTL 25%, max wind 12 m/s
- **Progressive confirmation**: Pre-flight, Pre-arm, Mid-flight exceptions, 10s timeout default hold

- **Out of scope**: Multi-UAV, BVLOS, cloud-only autonomy, emergency services

## 02-SAFETY-FAILSAFE (6 files)

### 6. battery_power_management.md (577 lines)
**Summary**: Complete guide to LiPo battery safety, PX4 battery parameters, power system design for RPi4, current sensing calibration, and operational procedures.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- LiPo voltage: 4.20V full, 3.85V storage, 3.50V low, 3.30V critical, 3.00V damage threshold
- PX4 battery estimation methods: (1) voltage, (2) load-compensated voltage, (3) voltage+current integration (recommended)
- PX4 failsafe: BAT_LOW_THR=0.30 (30% warning), BAT_CRIT_THR=0.20 (20% RTL), BAT_EMERGEN_THR=0.10 (10% land), COM_LOW_BAT_ACT=2 (Land)
- COM_ARM_BAT_MIN=0.40 (40% required to arm)
- RPi 4 requires 5V/3A min (15-25W), switching BEC recommended (5V/5A, >90% efficiency)
- Power distribution: Main battery -> Power Module -> FC + BEC (5V/5A) -> RPi4
- Wire gauge: 22AWG for <5A, 14AWG for >40A
- Cold weather: capacity drops to 70% at -10C, increase BAT_LOW_THR to 35-40%
- Battery maintenance: deep discharge test every 50 cycles, replace if <80% capacity at 100 cycles
- RTL reserve calculation: hover current x RTL time x 1.5 safety factor

**Action Items:**
- Calibrate voltage divider and current sensor before first flight
- Set conservative battery thresholds for RPi4 payload overhead
- Implement pre-flight voltage checklist

### 7. edge_case_scenarios.md (1754 lines)
**Summary**: Comprehensive failure mode analysis covering network, sensor, control, and LLM failures with decision trees and recovery procedures.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- Network failures: WiFi drop (CRITICAL, HOLD->RTL sequence), intermittent packet loss (adaptive: 5/15/25/40% thresholds), half-open connections (stale detection via heartbeat timestamps), UDP amplification attack (rate limiting, MAVLink signing)
- GPS spoofing/jamming: Position jump >10m detection, multi-sensor validation (IMU, optical flow, magnetometer, barometer), 30s dead reckoning limit, fallback hierarchy: GPS+RTK -> GPS+IMU -> IMU dead reckoning -> optical flow -> manual
- Magnetometer interference: Field strength deviation >0.5x triggers alert, GPS COG for heading when moving
- Barometer ground effect: <3m altitude, switch to rangefinder primary, EKF2_HGT_MODE=1
- Camera obscuration: Quality metrics (luminance <20 or >230), auto-exposure 2s timeout, fallback to non-visual navigation
- Motor failure: RPM deviation >30% triggers emergency, asymmetric thrust compensation
- LLM failure modes: Infinite loop (iteration cap 10), hallucinated commands (safety validation), tool call errors (graceful fallback), context overflow (sliding window)
- Multi-sensor fusion: Position source priority (GPS+IMU -> optical flow -> dead reckoning)
- Packet loss thresholds: warning 5%, degraded 15%, critical 25%, emergency 40%

**Action Items:**
- Implement all decision trees as GuardianProcess modules
- Add GPS spoofing detection with multi-sensor cross-validation
- Build LLM failure injection test suite

### 8. failsafe_hierarchy.md (832 lines)
**Summary**: Complete 4-layer failsafe hierarchy for the RPi5-based system with PX4 native failsafes, Guardian Process, LLM reactions, and operator override.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- **4-Layer Architecture**:
  - Layer 1 (PX4 Hard Reflexes): Watchdog <500ms, attitude limits, hardware failsafe, battery <3.0V/cell, motor failure RPM deviation >30%. Latency <10ms. Non-overridable.
  - Layer 2 (Guardian Process on RPi): Heartbeat monitor (LLM process, PX4 connection), command validator (speed >15m/s, altitude <2m, distance >500m rejected), state consistency, VIO sanity, resource monitor (CPU >90%, temp >80C, RAM >95%), network monitor. State machine: NORMAL -> GUARDED -> INTERVENTION.
  - Layer 3 (LLM Reactions): Navigation full authority within geofence+30% battery, speed limited to 10m/s, altitude 5-120m AGL, emergency RTL/land only by Guardian/PX4
  - Layer 4 (Operator Override): RC mode switch absolute, GCS override high, emergency stop absolute, geofence override conditional
- Guardian actions: Level 1 (log), Level 2 (override LLM, hold), Level 3 (disable LLM, RTL), Level 4 (land), Level 5 (disarm)
- Decision matrices: RTL vs Land vs Hold based on battery/RC/position/LLM status
- Complete state machine: INIT -> DISARMED -> ARMED -> MANUAL/POSITION/OFFBOARD/RTL/LAND/HOLD -> EMERGENCY
- Offboard loss: COM_OF_LOSS_T=0.5s, COM_OBL_RC_ACT=3 (Return)
- RC loss: COM_RC_LOSS_T=0.5s, NAV_RCL_ACT=2 (Return), COM_RCL_EXCEPT=4 (ignore RC loss in Offboard)
- Geofence: GF_MAX_HOR_DIST=1000m, GF_MAX_VER_DIST=150m, GF_ACTION=3 (Return)
- Landing below 5m AGL cannot be aborted

**Action Items:**
- Implement GuardianProcess as separate async task on RPi
- Configure all PX4 parameters per recommended values
- Build state machine with explicit transition validation

### 9. first_flight_procedures.md (657 lines)
**Summary**: Systematic progressive first flight test protocol with checklists, data collection requirements, and abort criteria.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- 5-phase progressive testing: Phase 0 (ground setup), Phase 1 (tethered, props off), Phase 2 (tethered, props on), Phase 3 (hover test at 1m), Phase 4 (position hold), Phase 5 (waypoint square)
- Pre-flight checklist: structural integrity, propulsion, electrical, sensors, software verification, calibration, safety systems, failsafe verification, control link test
- Directional verification: roll right, pitch forward, yaw right, throttle up - all confirmed before untethered flight
- Vibration thresholds: <15 m/s^2 excellent, 15-30 good, 30-50 acceptable (monitor), >50 UNACCEPTABLE
- Control loop performance: setpoint tracking <5 deg error, no sustained oscillations, overshoot <20%, settling <2s
- Abort criteria: control reversal, oscillations >20 deg, motor failure, uncommanded yaw >45 deg/s, rapid altitude loss, smoke/fire, structural failure, flyaway (all immediate kill switch)
- Controlled aborts: vibration >40 m/s^2, position drift >3m, GPS HDOP >3, battery sag >0.3V, wind >8 m/s
- GPS requirements: 8+ satellites, HDOP <2.0
- Location: 10m radius clear area, wind <5 m/s, visibility >1km, 3 personnel (pilot, spotter, data recorder)

**Action Items:**
- Print checklist and follow strictly for first flight
- Collect vibration, control loop, battery, GPS accuracy, and RC range data
- Complete all 5 phases before attempting autonomous flight

### 10. geofencing_hard_limits.md (1528 lines)
**Summary**: Detailed geofencing system research and hard limits design that cannot be overridden by LLM, user commands, or mission planning.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- PX4 geofence types: Cylindrical (basic, GF_MAX_HOR_DIST/GF_MAX_VER_DIST), Polygonal (inclusion/exclusion zones via MAVLink commands)
- GF_ACTION values: 0=None, 1=Warning, 2=Hold, 3=Return (recommended), 4=Terminate, 5=Land
- **Hard Limits (immutable, validated before any tool execution)**:
  - Altitude ceiling: 120m AMSL (400ft FAA), 10m buffer, LAND_IMMEDIATE on breach
  - Distance limit: 500m horizontal, 50m buffer, RTL on breach
  - Battery RTL: 25% trigger, 10% emergency land, 5% terminate, cannot be overridden
  - Geofence buffer: 10m min from fence, breach action RTL (cannot be None/Warn), exclusion zones absolute
- Commands that MUST be rejected: exceed altitude, exceed distance, disable geofence, low battery mission, exclusion entry, disable termination
- Validation architecture: LLM -> Command Parser -> Safety Validation Layer (HARD LIMITS) -> Vehicle Interface
- HardLimits dataclass (frozen=True): max_altitude_m=120, max_distance_m=500, min_battery_rtl_pct=25, geofence_buffer_m=10
- Pre-flight validation: all waypoints must be inside fence envelope
- Direct PX4 parameter enforcement: GF_MAX_VER_DIST=120, GF_MAX_HOR_DIST=500, GF_ACTION=3, BAT_CRIT_THR=0.25

**Action Items:**
- Implement GeofenceValidator class with mission validation and real-time position monitoring
- Set all PX4 parameters for hardware enforcement
- Create rejection response format for LLM-originated violations

### 11. safety_standards.md (1629 lines)
**Summary**: Research and compliance reference covering applicable standards (DO-178C, ASTM F38, ISO 21384, FAA Part 107), code quality gates, and safety validation checklists for UAV software with LLM integration.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- Standards hierarchy: DO-178C (DAL C/B for flight control, emergency response, RC override), ASTM F38 (autonomy levels), ISO 21384 (SORA risk assessment), FAA Part 107
- ASTM F38 autonomy classification: Level 3 (conditional autonomy) = HIGH risk for LLM integration; Level 5 (full autonomy) = PROHIBITED
- DAL assignments: Flight control loop DAL C/B, Emergency response DAL B, RC override DAL B, LLM interface DAL D, Telemetry DAL E
- Code quality: 100% statement/decision coverage for DAL C, MC/DC for DAL B
- Static analysis: pylint, SonarQube/Coverity, MISRA C:2012 for embedded, CERT C
- Required DO-178C documents: PSAC, SDP, SVP, SCMP, SQAP, SRD, SDD, SVCP, SVR, SAS
- Safety reflex independence validation: hardware isolation, software isolation, timing isolation (<10ms), power isolation, communication isolation (one-way safety bus)
- LLM failure tests: failure injection, command flooding, malicious commands, latency tests (<50ms safety reflex regardless of LLM load)
- FAA Part 107: 400ft AGL, 100mph max, VLOS required, Remote ID broadcast, >250g registration
- SORA risk assessment: 6 SAIL levels, SAIL V requires DO-178C DAL D

**Action Items:**
- Follow DAL C/B standards for safety-critical code
- Implement LLM failure injection test suite
- Maintain DO-178C documentation trail

## 03-SOFTWARE-ARCHITECTURE (9 files)

### 12. AGENT_CONNECTION_QUICKSTART.md (188 lines)
**Summary**: Quick-start guide for connecting any MCP-compatible agent to the drone, with setup instructions for Claude Code, OpenCode, Python scripts, and troubleshooting.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- Claude Code: `claude mcp add drone-server --command "python scripts/run_mcp_server.py" --transport stdio`
- OpenCode: skill.json config in `~/.opencode/skills/drone-control/`
- Python custom: MCP ClientSession with StdioServerParameters
- Environment variables: FIREWORKS_API_KEY (required), DRONE_MAVLINK_URL=udp://:14540, DRONE_USE_KIMI=true, DRONE_FRAME_INTERVAL=3.0, DRONE_CONFIRMATION_TIMEOUT=10
- Universal test script: `python scripts/test_agent_connection.py`
- Common commands: arm_and_takeoff, goto_gps, fly_body_offset, land, rtl, get_telemetry, capture_frame, abort_mission

**Action Items:**
- Create run_mcp_server.py entry point
- Create test_agent_connection.py universal test

### 13. logging_telemetry.md (1719 lines)
**Summary**: Comprehensive logging architecture covering PX4 flight data logging (uORB topics, ULog format), application logging design (structured LLM decision logs, telemetry snapshots), forensic requirements, and Python implementation.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- PX4 essential uORB topics: vehicle_attitude (250Hz), vehicle_local_position (50Hz), estimator_status (50Hz), actuator_outputs (250Hz), battery_status (1Hz), failsafe_flags (5Hz), offboard_control_mode (5Hz)
- SD card: 8-32GB Class 10, ~1-5 MB/min, ULog binary format
- LLM decision log schema: decision_id, timestamp, flight_uuid, decision_type, position/attitude/velocity, prompt_version, context_summary, raw_response, parsed_command, confidence_score, safety_check_passed, outcome, command_ack
- TelemetrySnapshot: complete system state captured with each command
- Vision metrics: FPS, inference time, detection count, tracking stability
- Network quality: heartbeat latency, message rate, packet loss, signal strength
- Log correlation: match LLM decisions (1-10Hz) with PX4 flight data (250Hz) by timestamp
- IncidentAnalyzer class for post-incident reconstruction
- Python logging: RotatingFileHandler (100MB, 10 backups), structured JSON logs
- Async logging for telemetry: background writer thread, bounded queue
- Log retention: 90 days standard, 365 days for incidents, permanent for crash events

**Action Items:**
- Configure PX4 SDLOG custom topics file
- Implement LLMDecisionLog dataclass and structured logger
- Build IncidentAnalyzer for log correlation

### 14. mcp_agent_agnostic_design.md (501 lines)
**Summary**: Detailed design for the agent-agnostic MCP (Model Context Protocol) architecture that works with any MCP-compatible AI agent.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- Architecture: Agent Layer (Claude/OpenCode/Hermes/any) -> MCP Wire Protocol -> Drone MCP Server -> MAVLink -> Hardware
- MCP = "USB-C for AI agents" - universal tool-calling interface
- Tool interface (same for all agents): Flight control (8 tools), Telemetry (3 tools), Vision (2 tools), Planning (1 tool)
- Confirmation workflow is agent-driven: server provides data, agent handles user interaction
- Two configs: Full Intelligence (Kimi enabled, LLM planning, vision frames to Kimi) vs Direct Control (no LLM, template-based planning, YOLO detections only)
- Safety independent of agent: GuardianProcess + 20Hz heartbeat continue regardless of agent connectivity
- Agent is NOT in the safety chain - only in the decision chain
- Future: IDEs, mobile apps, voice assistants, enterprise platforms (Slack/Teams bots)

**Action Items:**
- Implement MCP server with stdio transport
- Create agent-specific setup guides
- Test with multiple agents (Claude Code, OpenCode, custom Python)

### 15. mission_planning_patterns.md (1677 lines)
**Summary**: Standardized mission templates (lawnmower search, spiral search, orbit, perimeter scan, moving target follow), safety envelopes, and dynamic replanning patterns.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- Templates: search_lawnmower (parallel tracks), search_spiral (Archimedean spiral), orbit_circle (circular with camera targeting), perimeter_scan (sequential waypoints), moving_target_follow (dynamic tracking with prediction)
- Safety envelopes: max distance from home (phase-based overrides: 100m takeoff, 5000m mission), max altitude (per-phase), geofence shapes (circle/ellipse/polygon), battery reserve envelope
- LLM mission understanding: natural language -> intent classification -> template selection -> parameter extraction -> waypoint generation -> validation
- Dynamic replanning: trigger events (obstacle, weather, battery, GPS degradation, new target), replanning strategies (reroute/abort/scale/loiter), replanning constraints (max 3 replans per mission, 30s cool-down)
- Mission validation: self-intersection check, max total distance, geofence compliance, battery feasibility
- Moving target follow: 5Hz update frequency, linear extrapolation prediction (5s horizon), lost target actions (hover/search/return/abort)

**Action Items:**
- Implement mission template generators
- Build LLM intent classifier for template selection
- Create dynamic replanning engine with trigger event handlers

### 16. network_reliability.md (712 lines)
**Summary**: Detailed analysis of WiFi MAVLink reliability covering UDP vs TCP, WiFi failure modes, heartbeat redundancy, link quality monitoring, and graceful degradation patterns.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- UDP recommended over TCP for MAVLink (no head-of-line blocking, heartbeat-based liveness)
- WiFi latency: 2.4GHz 5-15ms RTT, 5GHz 3-10ms RTT (ideal), congestion 50-200ms spikes
- Recommendation: 5GHz primary (channels 149-161, non-DFS), 2.4GHz fallback
- Practical range: 100-300m reliable control (2.4GHz), 50-150m (5GHz)
- Heartbeat redundancy: Flight controller (sys 1, comp 1), Companion computer (sys 1, comp 191), GCS backup (sys 255, comp 190)
- Link quality scoring: Excellent (90-100, <1% loss, <20ms), Good (70-89), Fair (50-69), Poor (30-49), Critical (0-29)
- 4-level graceful degradation: FULL (1080p@30fps, all messages@10Hz) -> REDUCED_VIDEO (720p@15fps) -> ESSENTIAL_ONLY (video OFF, heartbeat+attitude+position@2Hz) -> CRITICAL (heartbeat only, emergency only)
- Packet loss detection: MAVLink sequence number tracking (8-bit wrapping), timestamp validation, rate monitoring
- Half-open detection: transmitting but not receiving, force reconnect on stale >5s
- Socket tuning: 64KB recv/send buffers, non-blocking, SO_REUSEADDR

**Action Items:**
- Implement LinkMonitor and LinkQualityMonitor classes
- Build GracefulDegradation manager
- Configure socket parameters for MAVLink

### 17. ollama_tool_patterns.md (2435 lines)
**Summary**: Comprehensive patterns for local LLM-driven drone control using Ollama with Llama 3, ReAct agent pattern, safety validation layers, and guardrails. (Note: Local Ollama is archived/superseded by Kimi K2.5, but patterns remain relevant.)

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- Ollama tool calling via `/api/chat` with tools parameter (OpenAI-compatible format)
- Tool definition: name, description, JSON Schema parameters
- Multi-turn conversation loop: LLM decides -> system executes -> results fed back
- Structured output via Pydantic schemas (DroneActionResponse, TelemetryData)
- ReAct pattern: Thought -> Action -> Observation loop with max_iterations=10
- Safety system architecture: ToolDefinition layer -> SafetyValidator (altitude/speed/distance/battery/geofence checks) -> ExecutableGuard -> ParallelValidation
- GuardianProcess: monitors LLM actions, can override/reject commands, state machine (ACTIVE/MONITORING/INTERVENING/EMERGENCY)
- LLM guardrails: confidence threshold, action repetition detection, context window management, response format enforcement
- Error recovery: timeout (retry with backoff), malformed response (re-prompt), safety violation (reject+log+fallback), system state change (abort+reassess)
- Safety checklist per action: battery, altitude, distance, geofence, weather, communication link, no-fly zones, speed limits
- (Local Ollama archived in favor of Kimi K2.5 cloud, but the safety/validation/guardrail patterns are directly reusable)

**Action Items:**
- Adapt ReAct agent pattern for Kimi K2.5 API
- Implement GuardianProcess with safety validation layer
- Build LLM guardrails (confidence, repetition, context management)

### 18. python_asyncio_patterns.md (2383 lines)
**Summary**: Concrete asyncio design patterns for safety-critical drone control including real-time task prioritization, event loop blocking prevention, memory management, and fault recovery.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- Priority scheduling: CRITICAL (0, heartbeat/emergency), HIGH (1, telemetry/safety), MEDIUM (2, vision/state), LOW (3, LLM/logging), BACKGROUND (4, analytics)
- DeadlineScheduler: precise periodic execution with call_at()/call_later(), jitter tolerance monitoring
- PreciseSleeper: compensates for execution time to prevent cumulative drift in 20Hz heartbeat
- CPU-bound work isolation: ComputeIsolator with ThreadPoolExecutor/ProcessPoolExecutor, YOLO timeout 100ms, LLM timeout 5s
- Preallocated buffer pools: FrameBufferPool (416x416x3 uint8), DetectionResultPool, prevent GC pressure in long-running loops
- Graceful shutdown: ordered task cancellation (heartbeat last), resource cleanup, state persistence
- State machine integration: async state transitions, transition validation, event-driven updates
- Watchdog patterns: SafetyWatchdog with timeout, health check-in, independent monitor verification
- Memory leak prevention: preallocated pools, bounded queues, circular buffers, weak references
- Fault recovery: never exit process mid-flight, circuit breaker pattern, error quarantine, state recovery from PX4

**Action Items:**
- Implement PriorityScheduler for all async tasks
- Use PreciseSleeper for heartbeat timing
- Set up ComputeIsolator for YOLO and LLM calls
- Create preallocated buffer pools for vision pipeline

### 19. ros2_vs_mavsdk.md (1054 lines)
**Summary**: Architecture comparison between ROS2 (DDS middleware) and direct MAVSDK for Project Avatar, with a clear recommendation for direct MAVSDK in Stage 1-2.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- **Decision: Direct MAVSDK for Stage 1-2** (lower latency 1-4ms vs 3-20ms, simpler debugging, 10MB vs 500MB+ footprint)
- ROS2 latency breakdown: DDS serialization 0.2-0.5ms + transport 0.5-1ms + uXRCE agent 1-3ms = 3-20ms total
- MAVSDK latency: asyncio switch ~0.01ms + library 0.1-0.5ms + MAVLink serialization ~0.1ms + network 0.5-1ms = 1-4ms total
- ROS2 becomes necessary only for: multi-drone coordination, complex perception (SLAM, nav stack), multi-process architecture
- MAVSDK pattern: shared TargetPosition dataclass, asyncio.Lock for thread safety, dedicated heartbeat task at 20Hz, pre-stream setpoints before entering offboard
- MAVSDK offboard control: PositionNedYaw setpoints, VelocityNedYaw for velocity mode
- PX4 requires continuous setpoint stream at >=2Hz (ideally 10-20Hz); stream stops -> PX4 exits offboard -> failsafe

**Action Items:**
- Use direct MAVSDK architecture for Stage 1-2
- Implement shared target position with asyncio.Lock
- Pre-stream setpoints for 1 second before entering offboard mode

### 20. tool_schema_design.md (1701 lines)
**Summary**: Complete JSON tool schema definitions for all LLM-drone control tools across all stages, with safety constraints, preconditions, error handling, and mutex rules.

**Key Facts/Decisions:**
- **Stage 1 Tools**: arm_and_takeoff (altitude 1-120m), goto_gps (speed 1-20 m/s), fly_body_offset (max 100m), set_velocity (max 25 m/s), hold (1-3600s), land, rtl
- **Stage 2 Tools**: start_search_pattern (lawnmower/spiral), lock_target, center_target_in_frame, capture_frame, get_detected_objects, abort_mission
- **Stage 3 Tools**: maintain_offset (distance-based), evaluate_path_safety, aim_gimbal, trigger_payload
- Each tool has: name, description, parameters (JSON Schema), returns, preconditions (required_state, mutex, checks), idempotent flag, estimated_duration
- Safety constraints embedded in schema: min/max values, required states, geofence checks, battery minimums
- Mutex rules: only one navigation command active at a time (goto_gps mutex with hold, land, rtl)
- Error responses include: code, message, recoverable flag, suggested_action
- Execution tracking: UUID execution_id for all commands, progress_percent for navigation
- Preconditions: battery_min_percent, gps_fix_required, geofence_check, no_fly_zone_check, altitude_bounds_check

**Action Items:**
- Implement all tool schemas as Python dataclasses/Pydantic models
- Build tool validation layer with precondition checking
- Create execution tracking with UUID-based logging
- Implement mutex enforcement for concurrent command prevention

## CROSS-CUTTING THEMES

1. **Safety Architecture**: 4-layer (PX4 hard reflexes -> Guardian Process -> LLM reactions -> RC override). Safety never depends on LLM or cloud connectivity.

2. **LLM Strategy**: Kimi K2.5 via Fireworks AI (cloud, 200 tok/s, multimodal). Local Ollama archived. Cost ~$0.10/flight.

3. **MCP Agent-Agnostic**: One MCP server works with any MCP client (Claude Code, OpenCode, Hermes, custom scripts). stdio transport.

4. **Hardware Stack**: MacBook Pro M3 (ground station), Raspberry Pi 4 (companion, 20Hz heartbeat), Pixhawk 6C (PX4), RPi cam or USB camera, phone cellular for Kimi.

5. **Budget**: ~$400-500 for drone hardware (used parts aggressively).

6. **Timeline**: Phase 0.5 (3 weeks SITL) -> Stage 1 (April-May, control spine) -> Stage 2 (June-July, vision) -> Stage 3 early (Aug-Sept, depth/payload bench).

7. **Hard Limits**: Altitude 120m, Distance 500m, Battery RTL 25%, Geofence buffer 10m - all immutable and enforced at tool validation layer AND PX4 parameters.
